file_name: Aishasiddhika and Jenovajosephine - 2024 - Skin cancer detection using image processing.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses transfer learning models for feature extraction and classification of skin cancer lesions (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and normal skin) from images. The methodology involves image preprocessing, transfer learning for feature extraction, and classification based on extracted features, trained on a dataset of 3600 images over 70 epochs.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including a proposed system architecture, methodology (image preprocessing, feature extraction using transfer learning, and classification), and results (training on 3600 images for 70 epochs with accuracy and loss graphs). It introduces a new web application for skin cancer detection rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: SKIN CANCER DETECTION USING IMAGE PROCESSING
journal: International Research Journal of Modernization in Engineering Technology and Science
brief_summary: This study presents an automated web application for the early detection and classification of skin cancer lesions, specifically distinguishing between melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and normal skin. The system utilizes image processing techniques, including resizing to 224x224 pixels and RGB conversion, followed by feature extraction via transfer learning models. The model was trained on a dataset of 3600 images, with 2880 images for training and 720 for testing, over 70 epochs. Evaluation results indicate that training accuracy increased within the first 10 epochs and remained constant, while loss decreased significantly after the initial phase. The proposed tool aims to improve diagnostic precision and accessibility, particularly in regions with limited dermatological services, by providing fast and non-invasive analysis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three distinct categories the system distinguishes skin lesions into, used in the classification task.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, or normal skin
all_task_types.details: The system performs image classification to distinguish skin lesions into three distinct categories: melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and normal skin. The methodology involves feature extraction via transfer learning and calculating the likelihood (probability) of each category, selecting the class with the highest probability as the outcome. The task is explicitly described as 'Image Classification' in the keywords and methodology sections.

datasets_used.name: Unspecified Skin Cancer Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of skin lesion images used for training and testing the proposed skin cancer detection system. The specific source, collection name, or origin (e.g., ISIC, HAM10000, private hospital data) is not explicitly stated in the text.

use.training: Used to train the transfer learning model for 70 epochs.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the system's accuracy and generate detection rate graphs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Evaluation was based on training accuracy, loss, and detection rates over 70 epochs.

Transfer Learning Model.loss_trend: High loss initially, then rate of loss started decreasing after 10 epochs
Transfer Learning Model.detection_rate: Referenced in Fig 2 as 'different detection rates for 3 types of skin cancer lesions' but specific numerical values are not provided in the text

Unspecified Skin Cancer Image Dataset.notes: The paper reports qualitative trends for accuracy and loss over 70 epochs. Specific quantitative accuracy percentages or detection rate values are not explicitly stated in the text, only referenced via figures (Fig 2, Fig 3, Fig 4).

conclusions: The proposed automated system, utilizing image processing and transfer learning, successfully distinguishes between melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and normal skin conditions.
The system aims to augment diagnostic precision and facilitate early detection, particularly in regions with limited access to specialized dermatological services.
Training accuracy increased within the first 10 epochs and remained constant up to 70 epochs, while the loss rate decreased significantly after the initial phase.

The system is designed as a dynamic web application that can potentially evolve to perform real-time image analysis, expand classification capabilities to other cancer types, and become a mobile application for wider accessibility.
important_remarks: The specific source of the dataset (3600 images) is not reported, raising concerns about data provenance and potential selection bias.
No specific accuracy percentage or numerical detection rate values are provided in the text; results are only described qualitatively via graphs (Fig 2, 3, 4).
The paper does not report a validation split, using only training (2880) and testing (720) sets, which limits the robustness of the performance evaluation.
The specific transfer learning backbone model (e.g., ResNet, VGG) is not explicitly named, only referred to generally as 'transfer learning models' and 'AlexNet' in the literature survey.
No statistical significance tests (p-values, confidence intervals) were performed or reported.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, limiting generalizability to diverse skin types or clinical settings.
The system is presented as a web application prototype without evidence of clinical integration or prospective user studies.
